epigraph.text: Let all things be done decently and in order.
epigraph.quote: The theory of averages is the basis of all practical applications of statistics.

content.text:
While graphs reveal the overall shape of a distribution, we often need 
numerical summaries that capture its essential features. We begin with 
measures that describe the *center*—the typical or representative 
value.

The most common measure of center is the **arithmetic mean**, 
usually just called the "mean" or "average."

content.title: Sample Mean
content.content:
The **sample mean** of $n$ observations 
$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is
\[
 \xbar = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n} 
 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
\]

content.text:
The Greek letter $\mu$ (mu) denotes the mean of an entire population, 
while $\xbar$ denotes the mean of a sample. This distinction between 
**parameters** (population values) and 
**statistics** (sample values) will be central 
to statistical inference.

content.title: Computing the Mean
content.problem:
A small business records the number of customers each day for a week: 
23, 31, 28, 35, 42, 18, 27. Find the mean.

content.text:
The mean can be thought of as the *balance point* of the 
distribution. If we placed the data values on a number line and tried 
to balance it on a fulcrum, the mean is where it would balance.

The **median** is the middle value when observations 
are arranged in order.

content.title: Median
content.content:
To find the **median** of $n$ observations:

1. Arrange the observations in increasing order.

2. If $n$ is odd, the median is the middle value.

3. If $n$ is even, the median is the average of the two middle values.
The median has **location** $\frac{n+1}{2}$ in the ordered list.

content.title: Computing the Median
content.problem: Find the median of the customer data: 23, 31, 28, 35, 42, 18, 27.
content.solution:
First, order the data: 18, 23, 27, 28, 31, 35, 42.

With $n = 7$ observations, the median is at location $\frac{7+1}{2} = 4$.

The median is the 4th value: $\widetilde{x} = 28$.

Half the days had fewer than 28 customers; half had more.

content.text: Mean vs.\ Median: Sensitivity to Outliers

The mean and median often give similar values, but they can differ 
substantially when the distribution is skewed or contains outliers.

content.title: Resistance
content.content:
A statistic is **resistant** (or *robust*) 
if it is not strongly affected by extreme observations.

The median is resistant; the mean is not.

content.title: Effect of an Outlier
content.problem:
Suppose the busiest day in our customer data had 142 customers 
instead of 42: 23, 31, 28, 35, 142, 18, 27.
content.solution:
**New mean:**
\[
 \xbar = \frac{23 + 31 + 28 + 35 + 142 + 18 + 27}{7} 
 = \frac{304}{7} 
 \approx 43.4
\]
The mean jumped from 29.1 to 43.4—a substantial change.

**New median:** The ordered data are 18, 23, 27, 28, 31, 35, 142.

The median is still 28—unchanged by the extreme value.

content.title: Choosing Between Mean and Median
content.content:
- Use the **mean** when the distribution is roughly 
 symmetric without outliers, and when you want to incorporate 
 all data values.

- Use the **median** when the distribution is skewed 
 or contains outliers, or when you want a "typical" value 
 that is not influenced by extremes.
For skewed distributions: the mean is pulled toward the long tail.

content.text:
The **mode** is the most frequently occurring value. 
It is the only measure of center that can be used for categorical 
data.

For quantitative data, the mode is less useful when all values are 
distinct. For grouped data or distributions, we speak of *modal 
classes* or simply observe the peaks of the distribution.
*When $n$ is even, the median is defined as the average of two values.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler
marginNotes.text:
Mathematicians have tried in vain to this 
day to discover some order in the sequence of prime numbers, and we 
have reason to believe that it is a mystery into which the human mind 
will never penetrate.
